content.assessment: Quotas and outreach directly target compositional imbalance without changing the ranking/selection structure itself, meaning admitted disadvantaged students would enter the same high-achieving reference group associated with lower academic self-concept for equally able students in elite settings — a robust, well-documented risk. The proposal's own cited risk (contested value-added benefit of track placement) compounds this: if track placement offers limited genuine gain, admitted students may bear reference-group psychological costs without commensurate benefit. Early outreach (kindergarten-stage) aligns with self-determination theory's emphasis on early competence-building, but the core selection mechanism, and its associated performance-orientation pressures, remains unchanged for all students, disadvantaged and advantaged alike.
opportunities: If paired with school-quality improvements, expanded access could plausibly support competence and relatedness for previously excluded students, consistent with self-determination theory, though this is conditional and not guaranteed by quotas alone.
strengths: Early outreach beginning well before age-10 selection aligns with self-determination theory's emphasis on building competence and autonomy over developmental time rather than relying on last-minute exam preparation.
Directly targets compositional imbalance, which could, if successful, alter the peer-group composition dynamics relevant to reference-group effects.
threats: Admitted disadvantaged students placed via quota rather than exam performance could face labeling or expectancy effects from peers or teachers, an effect that is contested in magnitude but plausibly larger for stigmatized or low-SES students.
Without addressing underlying local-school distrust, quota-driven access may not shift parental sorting behavior, limiting systemic psychological impact.
weaknesses: Admitting more disadvantaged students into elite tracks without altering the tracks' internal culture or ranking intensity risks exposing them to the same big-fish-little-pond self-concept costs documented for equally able students in high-achieving settings.
Does not address performance-orientation and ranking pressures inherent in the preserved admissions exam and track structure.
Contested value of the track itself (as noted in the proposal's own risk list) undermines the psychological rationale for prioritizing access as the key lever.
